data.title: John Hancock Multimanager 2045 Lifetime Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary

  • Most asset categories produced solid returns in the final three months of 2025, reflecting the favorable backdrop for the world financial markets.
  • The fund posted a gain but slightly underperformed its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation contributed to performance, while underlying manager results detracted.

Market review and outlook

Global equities registered solid gains in the fourth quarter, helping the major, broad-based indexes record their third consecutive year of double-digit returns. Performance was uneven over the first half of the quarter due to concerns that AI-related stocks were in a bubble, but the market staged an impressive rebound and went on to achieve new all-time highs by year end. A continued decline in inflation enabled the U.S. Federal Reserve to enact two quarter-point interest rate cuts, boosting sentiment. In addition, corporate earnings were robust and world economic growth remained positive. Emerging- and developed-market international equities outperformed the United States, continuing a trend that was in place for the full year. Within the U.S. market, the value style outpaced growth as investors rotated toward opportunities outside of AI-related stocks.

Global bonds logged only slightly positive total returns amid a growing consensus that most central banks were largely finished easing policy. Credit-oriented market segments continued to outperform, primarily as a result of their yield advantage.

Contributors and detractors

The fund's overweight in equities versus bonds contributed to relative performance. The fund's overweight in developed-market international equities and corresponding underweight in the United States also contributed. We have favored the non-U.S. markets for some time based on their attractive relative valuations, and this aspect of our positioning paid off in both the quarter and the year. An overweight in emerging-market stocks, the best-performing segment of the global equity markets in the quarter, was a further plus. On the other hand, the allocation to defensive equities was a modest detractor at a time in which more speculative stocks were in favor. An overweight in U.S. mid caps also cost the fund some relative performance. Positioning in fixed income had a neutral effect on results. An allocation to emerging-market bonds performed well, as the asset class strongly outpaced its developed-market peers in an environment of falling interest rates and rising commodity prices. Conversely, a position in U.S. Treasury STRIPS detracted amid a broader increase in long-term bond yields.

No single area stood out as a key driver of underperformance for the fund's active managers; instead, the shortfall was primarily a result of very small differences for a wide range of individual holdings.

Portfolio changes

We retained diversified exposure across the full spectrum of the world financial markets, while also making some modest changes on the margin to capitalize on categories where we saw attractive valuations and higher long-term return potential. We remain confident in the fund's positioning as we move into 2026, based on our belief that diversification is likely to become increasingly important. For most of the past year, investors would have been rewarded by simply holding the narrow group of mega-cap U.S. technology-related stocks that continued to dominate performance. However, we began to see this backdrop evolve somewhat over the final three months of the year with a notable broadening of leadership. While it's impossible to say if this is a sign of a larger shift, we believe investors are better served by maintaining broad-based diversification rather than relying on another year of positive relative performance for a handful of companies. We think the fund is well positioned for these circumstances through its ability to seek opportunities across a wide range of asset classes.

data.title: Caleres Is Now Reasonably Priced, But Is Not Attractive After The Rally
data.description:
Caleres, Inc. (CAL) reported Q4 '25 results. The results had more negatives than positives, but the stock reacted very positively (up almost 20% on the day after the release). The reason behind the rally is probably that the company guided for a strong recovery in earnings next year.

Coming back to the quarter, it was not very good. Organic sales were basically flat, but the company still posted a lot of deleverage, to the point that adjusted operating earnings excluding Stuart Weitzman were almost zero

After that move, I believe the stock is now more or less fairly priced. At around $10.5 per share, and using management’s FY26 adjusted EPS guidance of $1.35 to $1.65, the stock trades at about 6.5x to 8x earnings. That looks optically cheap, but for a leveraged footwear brand with basically breakeven margins in many recent quarters, in a difficult consumer context, I think that is fair rather than attractive.

I maintain a Hold.

Q4 '25 results

The company's results need to be read with and without the acquisition of Stuart Weitzman. Fortunately, the company did provide most of the separate figures in the call.

The company reported Q4 sales up ~9% YoY, but this was almost entirely because of Stuart Weitzman. Excluding SW, sales were basically flat versus last year. Brand Portfolio sales were up 20% as reported, but only up 1.5% organically. Famous Footwear (the retail chain of the company) had sales down 1.2%, with comps basically flat.

Further, I worry a little about the quality of the sales in the flat organic picture. Management highlighted double-digit e-commerce growth for FF and good e-commerce growth for the brand portfolio. E-commerce is usually worse structurally for margin because of fulfillment and customer acquisition costs.

Excluding SW, adjusted gross margins were down 60bps. The more important point is inside Brand Portfolio, where adjusted gross margin excluding SW was down 120bps YoY, due to tariffs and markdown allowances. Famous Footwear, by contrast, held gross margin flat.

Management is guiding FY26 gross margin up 140bps to 180bps, driven by tariff mitigation and mix, not by any improvement in the tariff regime itself despite the recent volatility around these regulations. Most of that recovery likely has to come from pricing, which still leaves a question mark around consumer willingness to absorb it.

Because of the acquisition, SG&A delevered 370bps. Stuart Weitzman accounted for $39 million of additional expenses. But even excluding SW, SG&A still delevered 150bps. Adjusted operating earnings excluding Stuart Weitzman were just $0.5 million, or a 0.1% operating margin, versus 2.1% last year.

That is a significant fall in profitability on basically unchanged sales. My guess is that the e-commerce mix is part of the issue here, because the company is leaning harder into channels that usually carry more variable operating costs, showing up in SG&A (like customer acquisition).

Considering Stuart Weitzman, the company said on the call it expects to bring the brand to breakeven, from which I can infer it is currently generating losses, although I do not know if that includes the recent restructuring actions (like selling older inventory) or if it's on a more organic basis.

Finally, net interest expense rose by $1.2 million in the quarter, and year-end borrowings under the revolver were $297 million. The company has effectively leveraged the balance sheet in order to acquire Stuart Weitzman, and that brand is still not profitable. Management is guiding only to break even at SW in FY26. I criticized this capital allocation decision in previous articles, and I still think it was poor timing.

Valuation is fair

Management expects FY26 sales to be up LSD to MSD unadjusted, with Famous down LSD to flat, and the brands up LDD (unadjusted). Organically, management is guiding LSD/MSD growth in brands.

Still, the company has high hopes for next year. The bigger driver of earnings recovery is margins, with gross margin guided up 140bps to 180bps, and SG&A as a percentage of sales roughly flat. Again, the margin improvement is not based on tariff reductions, but rather 'tariff mitigations', corporate parlance for price hikes.

Considering interest expenses of around $18 million for the year (lower than what one gets from annualizing the 4Q25 figure and therefore indicative of deleveraging intentions), that gets the company to adjusted EPS of $1.35 to $1.65. This is clearly better than FY25 adjusted EPS of $0.61, or $1.19 excluding SW.

If we take the stock at about $10.5 after the release, that implies a P/E of about 6.5x to 8x on the FY26 adjusted EPS guide, and about 7x at the midpoint.

I think that is fair, but not cheap. The company is not in a particularly strong position. It has lost the balance sheet strength it used to have, the consumer environment is still challenging, and I do not have much confidence in management’s execution after the Stuart Weitzman acquisition. There is also a lot of operating leverage here, which means that a little bit of sales pressure can shrink earnings very quickly, as we just saw this quarter.

For those reasons, I maintain a Hold.

data.title: KB Home Q1 Preview: Weak Results Likely, But May Be Priced In
data.description:
Shares of KB Home (KBH) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 15% of their value. Over the past year, we have seen the housing market be persistently sluggish as consumers face affordability challenges. With its focus on first-time buyers, this challenge has been especially pronounced for KBH, and the war with Iran threatens to keep mortgage rates higher, eliminating a potential upside opportunity. I last covered shares in December, warning 2026 would be a challenging year, but valuation kept me at a “Hold.” With shares down 8% since then, I arguably should have rated shares a "Sell," and with KBH set to report earnings next week, now is a good time to preview results.

Q1 expectations are justifiably low

Currently, analysts are forecasting $0.54 of EPS in Q1, and pessimism has been building with 6 estimate cuts and not one increase. Now, the company has a track record of beating estimates with 12 beats over the past 16 quarters. With analysts having already lowered the bar so much, this may position the company for a modest beat. Now, Q1 is seasonally quiet, and there is likely to be more focus on what management projects for the key spring selling season in Q2 and into Q3.

I suspect that investors are already bracing for cautious results. Lennar (LEN), one of its biggest competitors, reported results last week that were unambiguously poor. It missed Q1 consensus, and it offered a downbeat outlook for Q2 orders, speaking to persistent headwinds from weak consumer sentiment. Given its reliance on first-time homebuyers, I am skeptical KBH can outperform peers in this selling environment. This negativity around the sector comes from a weak macro environment.

Affordability pressures limit housing market activity

The fundamental issue weighing on the housing market is that homes are not very affordable. Because prices are higher and interest rates are higher, potential buyers face dual pressures, and as a result, consumer incomes need to be 79% higher to afford the same house as six years ago. Now, this metric has improved ~6% from the peak as home prices have retraced modestly and interest rates are off their highs. Still, barring a larger readjustment, home-buying activity is likely to stay muted. This issue is especially pronounced for KBH, which caters to first-time buyers that don’t have equity in an appreciated house like someone trading up may have.

There are two ways to improve affordability: lower prices or lower rates. As a builder, KBH of course prefers lower rates to cutting home prices. In the second half of 2025, hopes built that a lower rate environment could be coming as the 30-year mortgage approached the critical 6.0% level. Ultimately, I think we need to see 5.75%-6.0% rates to really unlock demand. However, we have jumped 30bps from this level since the start of the Iran War.

With higher oil prices, Fed rate cuts appear less likely, and I now expect no more than 1 rate cut from 2 cuts previously. In general, we need to see the 10-year Treasury at or below 4% for the 30-year mortgage to be at or below 6%. That appears less and less likely between higher inflation and potential greater Treasury issuance needs to fund the war effort. Beyond just keeping rates higher, higher inflation and the associated geopolitical uncertainty only further exacerbate weak consumer sentiment and are likely to defer some homebuying season. As such, I fear this spring selling season may be yet another disappointment.

At the same time, pricing in its key Sun Belt markets has been weaker. While national home prices are flattish, there have been clear regional differences, with the Northeast outpacing the Sun Belt, in large part because construction has added substantial new supply to these growth markets. In fact, as you can see below, states like Florida and Texas have more inventory than pre-COVID. While other Sun Belt states like Georgia and the Carolinas have declines, they are modest relative to the drop-offs further north. Until this excess inventory is worked through, pricing will likely remain soggy. With mortgage rates limiting volumes, this is likely to be a prolonged process that extends through 2026.

Recent results were weak

Recapping its most recent results, in Q4, housing revenue fell by 16% to $1.7 billion as deliveries declined by 9% and pricing fell by 7%. On top of this, orders fell by 10% to 2,414. During 2025, the company was delivering more homes than it generated in orders, leading it to eat into its backlog. Its runway for continuing this practice is shortening as its backlog is down 37% over the past year to $1.4 billion, representing only about 3 months of inventory. With its backlog down so much, I expect the company will need to slow the pace of construction unless orders reaccelerate, which appears unlikely.

The other notable item in Q4 was a significant drop in gross margins to 17.8%, down 310bps from a year earlier. This was also down 110bps sequentially. In a weak demand environment, pricing is under pressure, and homebuilders need to make more concessions to move inventory. There is also no real capacity to pass any tariff-related costs on to consumers. Inventories ended the year up about 2%, and this rise in inventory despite lower homebuilding revenue is concerning. I am cautious that we may see further gross margin reductions in 2026 as it works to move houses given its thinner backlog cushion.

Separately, in January, KB Home announced it would be elevating Robert McGibney to the CEO role, while its current CEO will remain as Chairman of the Board. These changes will be effective March 1 and are indicative of a planned and orderly succession. With the current CEO remaining as chair for a period of time, there should be a smooth handover. I do not expect any changes in financial policy or strategy as a result of this succession.

Now, I would note that KBH has a 30.3% debt-to-capital ratio, which is consistent with the company’s operating target. Importantly, it has no maturities until 2027, reducing interest rate risk. This should enable the company to use free cash flow for shareholder returns. During 2025, KBH returned over $600 million to shareholders, and I expect the company to continue to execute buybacks. It reduced its share count by about 12% during 2025. Plus, shares offer a 1.9% dividend yield.

With an outlook cut likely, there is no rush to add KBH

Looking forward, in Q1, I expect deliveries and revenues to be at the lower portion of guidance, but near-term results should have less variability given the Q4 backlog position. I am most concerned about full-year guidance. I expect the company to slash at least the top end of deliveries and revenue, as the spring selling season is likely to be quieter than hoped for. I now see 10,500-11,500 deliveries and $4.9-$5.5 billion of revenue.

Back in December, I was targeting $5.15-$5.70 of EPS this fiscal year, and that was far too optimistic. Instead, I am now expecting KBH to earn $3.90-$4.60 this year. That leaves shares at about 12x earnings, which is not overly compelling for a cyclical business that is unlikely to see a material acceleration over the next 12 months. That said, book value ended 2025 at $61.75, so shares are now at a substantial discount to book value. This speaks to investor concerns about its higher inventory and whether it will need to take losses on its land position.

At this valuation, given my view that a recession is not the base case, I believe it is too low to sell KBH. That said, it is one of the more vulnerable large homebuilders, given its reliance on first-time buyers. With housing unlikely to rebound soon, I struggle to see a case for buying shares. If investors want to bet on housing, I would rather be in a name like Toll Brothers (TOL) with its higher-income customer base. I expect a guidance cut alongside Q1 earnings, but to a large extent this appears in the price. Given incrementally negative news, I see little rush to add and would want a drop into the mid-$40’s before buying.

data.title: Globus Medical: Great Value, Momentum, And Earnings Growth
data.description:
Globus Medical (GMED) is a medical device firm that focuses on improving quality of life for patients with musculoskeletal disorders. They offer solutions used in spine surgery, orthopedic trauma, joint reconstruction, and chronic pain treatment, serving hospitals and surgeons in 64 countries globally. GMED generates the majority of its revenue from implantable devices and surgical instruments, while getting also getting further into higher-value enabling technologies such as robotics, navigation systems, and imaging platforms.

The aging global population and increase in musculoskeletal disorders are playing a role in their financial strides taken over the last few years. The stock has been rocky over the last 5 years, returning just 41% to investors. In upbeat Q3 earnings back in November has spurred a charge from GMED, taking the stock from under $60 a share, now to around $85 today. The stock is down about 10% since another optimistic Q4 earnings beat to close out a strong 2025, with a bright outlook on 2026.

I have GMED as a buy, based on strong management guidance following a robust year in earnings and profit growth. The stock is undervalued and currently presents a strong case on the technical side too.

Earnings and Margin Expansion

GMED's trailing 12-month EPS has grown with almost every single quarter over the last 5 years, compounding at 22.8% annually. Q4 2025 reported $1.28 for adjusted EPS, an all-time high and over double the same quarter just two years ago. Revenue also beat expectations handily at $826 million, up 25% YoY. All 12 analysts on Seeking Alpha have upward revisions for GMED for both earnings and revenue in 2026. Year-end 2026 EPS estimate from Seeking Alpha is $4.47, which would be less than their current growth rate but still a solid 12%. I think with the growth they have seen organically and through acquisition, that closer to $5.00 2026 adjusted EPS is likely, especially based on management optimism.

Margins faded for several years for GMED, as acquisitional headwinds and cost inflation drove into profits. The firm has taken big steps over the last 6-7 quarters and has its operating margin back up above 20%. Their net income margin has also rebounded from lows of 4.5% in 2024, now above 18% for the last fiscal year. Net income itself is up over 5x from last year and was $537.9 million for the year.

Strategy, Guidance, & Acquisition

The biggest growth driver for GMED is through the acquisition of Nevro. This was finalized in 2025, bringing in the company as experts in the development of implantable spinal cord stimulation technology. Synergies with Nevro were smoother than anticipated, accretive to earnings by the nine-month marker, much quicker than the 15 months that were projected. An additional $293 million in revenue was brought in through the deal. It is still early in this transition, but so far, all signs indicate it is smooth sailing, which I expect to continue.

Management has made it clear in the 2025 10-K that their main forward-looking strategy is to leverage product development while increasing their U.S. sales force and international presence through organic and acquisitional efforts. In Q4, GMED's current business revenue grew 10% YoY. Nothing crazy, but still steady and a nice extension on the transactional growth.

Management has initial expectations for revenue to increase just under 10% in 2026 and has upped their prediction on adjusted EPS from $4.30-$4.40 to $4.40-$4.50 for this year. As I stated above, personally I think they can smash these earnings projections. They brought in 9 new products in 2025, most of which haven’t even received full attention yet in the market. Their fastest-growing segment, Enabling Technologies, had 19% higher sales in Q4 2025 and is their key to expansion in the ever-increasing robotic and AI side of the medical industry.

Technical Analysis

From a technical standpoint, GMED poses some very strong indicators to near-future success. The 50- to 200-day simple moving average is at its widest gap over the last year. Momentum is accelerating, and buyers are pushing the price higher. The stock is also not overbought either, as its Relative Strength Index is around 41, lower than its average over the last few years, and way off of 87 that it reached in November after Q3 earnings. Momentum is on their side right now.

Valuation

GMED is sitting at a big discount right now. The current PE of 22x is just under the sector median, but is well below its own historical ratio, even with the large recent price appreciation the stock has seen. It had a PE ratio above 40 for the majority of 2025. The sharp increase in earnings has led to the drop in valuation and leaves me believing this stock is selling much below its fair value. Price to book is 2.25x, below sector average and, again, compared to its own history, lower than it has been over almost the entire last decade.

Risks to Thesis

Much of GMED's recent revenue growth has been acquisition-driven, while underlying organic growth remains more modest. There still has been growth to its core business, but that alone would not have me sold on a buy for the stock. The integration with Nevro has been seamless so far, and I expect those efforts to continue. However, if sales take a step back or don’t reach forecasts with the new business moving forward, then GMED will lack the earnings and profit growth they saw in 2025.

In the healthcare space, there is risk for regulatory or government decisions affecting product delivery and usage. Musculoskeletal disorders have grown consistently, and with the aging population, I don’t see product delay as a likely issue for GMED.

Conclusion

I think GMED is in a really strong position on most accounts. Growth has been steady internally and strong through acquisition, which is a clear point of emphasis from management moving forward. Earnings and margins have taken great strides from lows about a year and a half ago. Their product development into more segments and more countries is advancing sales. Lastly, I truly see the company as undervalued right now and think that this recent 10% price drop is a good buy-in spot for investors.

data.title: I Acknowledge I Was Wrong After Watching Movado Group Surge (Upgrade)
data.description:
March 19th was a fantastic day for shareholders of watch company Movado Group, Inc. (MOV). Shares of the business shot up 9.5% in late afternoon trading after management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Revenue and earnings both came in above what analysts anticipated. Unfortunately, management refused to give guidance for 2027. But the overall trajectory of the company is getting better.

Back in August of last year, I made the decision to downgrade it from a Buy to a Hold. This was based on broader economic conditions, including what looked like an increasing likelihood of a recession for the U.S. I still believe that recession is coming. However, I was definitely premature in the downgrade. Shares are up 43.8% since then, dwarfing the 2.7% increase that the S&P 500 (SP500) saw. Based on fundamentals alone, the company is doing an exceptional job right now. And end market demand is improving. Add on top of this it's fortress balance sheet, and I think that upgrading it to a very soft Buy makes sense here.

A great quarter

No matter how you stack it, the final quarter of the 2026 fiscal year was a fantastic time for shareholders of Movado Group. The company reported revenue of $191.6 million. This was up 5.6% compared to the $181.5 million that the company reported a year earlier. This was a solid increase for the company. And it was driven by a couple of things. Most notably, its wholesale business reported a 25% surge in sales, with its hallmark website, Movado.com, experiencing an 8% rise in revenue while its Movado Company Stores grew by 9%. According to management, the company benefited from strong performance associated with its fashion watch and jewelry brands that can be tied back to product innovation and digital content.

The U.S., Mexico, and Europe, were particular bright spots for the company. In fact, here in the U.S., revenue skyrocketed 11.2%. That compares to just a 1% increase in international markets. It is important to note that the rise in international sales was only because of foreign currency fluctuations. On a constant currency basis, revenue actually dropped 5.9%. This weakness was because of issues in the Middle East. That region accounts for just 5.9% of overall revenue for the company. However, the recent conflict that we have seen was one reason why management made the decision to not issue any guidance for the 2027 fiscal year.

Even though management is not offering guidance at this time, there is evidence that the overall market for watches is improving. The WatchCharts Overall Market Index is a measure that looks at aftermarket sales of 300 quality watches. As the chart above illustrates, we have seen upward momentum when it comes to overall pricing for the index since I last wrote about the company in August of last year. Since then, the index has moved up 4.4%. And over the last year, it is up 6.9%. That is fantastic to see.

The revenue generated by the company exceeded analysts’ estimates by $9.6 million. This was not the only area of strength for the company. Earnings per share for the business shot up from $0.36 to $0.55. That reading came in $0.02 above what analysts were hoping to see. And it meant a rise in net profits from $8.1 million to $12.6 million. Adjusted earnings per share, meanwhile, grew from $0.51 to $0.57. This ended up being $0.03 above what analysts were hoping for and meant a rise in adjusted net profits from $11.5 million to $13 million. Other profitability metrics were mixed but generally positive. Operating cash flow shot up from $39.1 million to $56.6 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a nice surge from $7.8 million to $18.6 million. However, EBITDA for the business it did contract from $21.4 million to $16.1 million.

I will say that the overall improvement in profitability here was great considering that management had to contend with tariffs. According to management, they benefited from favorable channel and product mix, as well as increased leverage over lower fixed costs over higher sales, as well as foreign currency fluctuations, to absorb tariff expenses.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2026 in its entirety compared to 2025. Revenue, profits, adjusted profits, and almost every cash flow metric, increased for the company. The only exception to this was EBITDA, which remained flat year over year. As I mentioned already, management is not offering guidance for 2027. The hope here is that lower tariffs will at least help offset any weakness. Management incurred about $10 million of IEEPA tariff costs during the year. Those have since been ruled unconstitutional, but there is a global tariff rate of 15%. So there is some uncertainty about how much expense investors should anticipate. Management did mention that they expect demand to grow during the 2027 fiscal year. But beyond that, we have nothing to work with.

The good news is that the company ended 2026 with no debt and $230.5 million of cash and cash equivalents. That doesn't even include long term investments. This compares to a market capitalization of $542.6 million, which means that the enterprise value for the business is online 57.8% of its total market capitalization. This does have a big impact when it comes to the overall valuation of the company. In the chart below, you can see how the stock is priced using results from the 2025 and 2026 fiscal years. Although it's not exactly the cheapest when it comes to the price to operating cash flow approach, it is quite affordable when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach. Relative to other similar firms, it's priced well enough. On a price to operating cash flow basis, two of the three institutions that I compared it to on a price to operating cash flow basis ended up being cheaper than it is. But only one of these companies was cheaper on an EV to EBITDA basis.

When I first started looking at the data provided by management, I was a bit critical of inventories. Although it's nice to have stock on hand in the event that demand is robust, I personally believe the U.S. is nearing a recession. Last year, about 43.3% of its revenue came from the domestic market. So this would not be immaterial to the company if I turn out to be right. In particular, I would expect that the 53 retail outlet locations in the U.S. stand to experience weakness if economic conditions deteriorate further. So having increased inventory on hand can pose some risk that management might be forced to ultimately slash prices in order to get inventory off the shelves. The good news is that the increase from $156.7 million to $158.3 million was driven entirely by higher prices caused by tariffs. Management pegged the increase at $3.1 million. Without that factored in, inventories would have fallen by about 1% year over year.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available at this moment, I think that I was premature and downgrading Movado Group from a Buy to a Hold. I believed that the market for watches would remain weaker for longer. I do still think that there is some risk here associated with broader economic conditions. But with the strong balance sheet that the company has and the improvements that we have seen in the industry and with the company itself, I believe that upgrading it to a very soft Buy makes sense here.

data.title: Ardmore Shipping: If Returns Don't Improve, A Takeover Is The Best Path (Downgrade)
data.description:
I have covered Ardmore Shipping Corporation (ASC) before, where I outlined the investment thesis in detail and explained why I considered it an interesting buy. Since the last article, the stock has surpassed my previous fair value. While I have increased the fair value further, I am downgrading the stock to "Hold."

Background

Ardmore is a product and chemical tanker operator focused on the MR segment. After the acquisition of three vessels, its fleet consists of 19 MRs and 6 chemical tankers, with an average age of around 10 years.

The company was mainly spot, but in the last quarter it has been increasing its chartered-out coverage. In Q3, they chartered out four vessels, and in Q4, one more. While it wouldn’t surprise me if they chartered out some extra vessels, given the Q4 conference call commentary, I still expect them to remain primarily spot focused.

I mean, in terms of revenue days for the year ahead, it's still 82% market exposure. So we're still a predominant spot player and for good reason. So I wouldn't want this to be misinterpreted as a full sort of risk-off move. But we always like to look at what we do within the company across the whole portfolio, buying ships, locking in some high-quality time charters out

Ardmore Shipping has a clear capital allocation policy, and while I was optimistic about a possible increase in the payout ratio, given the Q4 comments, I don’t expect any change in the short term.

The lowest priority continues to be returning capital to shareholders, as the company currently returns just one-third of adjusted earnings despite being almost net cash.

Instead, the money has been invested in growth and upgrades to the fleet. This is visible in recent decisions where the company completed a heavy drydock and upgrade cycle, including tank coating improvements that allow for more cargo flexibility.

Growth has also been selective with the acquisition of three MR vessels. Management has demonstrated that it looks for opportunistic transactions.

Finally, on the deleveraging front, the company refinanced its debt at better terms and fully redeemed preferred shares, reducing breakeven levels.

Financial Position and Valuation

Ardmore has a rock-solid financial position with $46.8 million in cash and $127 million in debt, implying leverage of around 17%. The increase in leverage is due to the acquisition of the three MRs.

This leverage is very low for an asset-intensive business like shipping and results in one of the lowest breakeven levels in the sector, around $11,700 per day. At the same time, it provides significant operating leverage. According to the company, every $10,000 increase in TCE rates adds approximately $1.90 to annual EPS.

Q4 results were in line with expectations, with an EPS of $0.28 and a $0.09 dividend. Given the guidance and rates evolution for Q1, I expect considerably better results with at least $0.5 EPS and $0.15 dividend, representing around a 4% yield. Despite this, the yield continues to be considerably lower than that of some peers like TORM (TRMD), as they only pay one-third of earnings as dividends, while TORM offers a much higher payout. This justifies some valuation discount, and I consider that Ardmore should be acquired by some of the bigger players.

Regarding valuation, while the NAV per share has appreciated since the previous article, the discount remains. I still believe that the company should be valued higher than its current valuation, but without higher dividends, it will be difficult to trade above NAV.

Assuming a 0.9 times NAV gives a $17 fair value, representing close to 20% upside. But I believe that the company should be acquired by a competitor to realize the full potential.

Risks

With the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, China has banned product exports.

With global refineries reducing their runs due to the lack of oil, this could create a deficit of products to be exported, impacting rates.

Another major risk is if the United States decides to implement a similar ban. This will be highly negative, as many tankers in general and MR in particular will remain unemployed due to the lack of products to transport.

For now, rates are elevated, but if the situation changes, they could drop quickly.

Conclusion

Ardmore Shipping remains cheaper than its peers, but for good reasons. I believe that until the company improves its payout ratio, it will remain below NAV. This is a perfect setup for a peer to make an accretive transaction and acquire the company, but for now, there has been no interest.

Given the possible risks in the short term and the lack of improvement in shareholder returns, I am downgrading the stock to a "Hold."

However, if rates remain at current levels, I believe that the stock is still interesting.

data.title: If Cuba Rebuilds Its Power Grid, Which Companies Could Be Involved?
data.description:
An energy failure

Cuba is facing an energy crisis. While a lot of this is due to the oil being cut off from Venezuela, the poor condition of the grid in general is to blame as well.

More on the situation:

Cuba is slowly restoring power to 10 million people after its national electrical grid collapsed March 16, a blackout caused by an obsolete power system coupled to an oil blockade imposed by President Donald Trump.

The cause of the failure was still being investigated. Cuban officials said they were looking at electrical transmission problems and not a major power plant breakdown.

The island’s decrepit power system uses aging thermal power plants from the former Soviet Union. The system uses about 100,000 barrels of oil a day for essential services.

While it is unfortunate that the people of Cuba have to suffer through this, there is speculation that the US and the Trump administration may step in to address the energy challenges of the island nation. This might just be a forced opening up to US interests, similar to Venezuela. Whatever happens, investors have to contemplate who in the market (SPY)(DIA)(QQQ)(VOO) may be involved in a potential rebuild and modernization of the Cuban grid.

Any participation would likely enter a bidding process, and I'd like to emphasize that there is zero guarantee that any company mentioned would have an inside track to a winning bid.

The AES Corporation (AES), which operates in Chile, the Dominican Republic, El Salvador, Puerto Rico, and Mexico, for instance, would be a US utility company with regional businesses already. Unfortunately, the company appears to be going private, being taken over by a BlackRock (BLK)-led consortium.

Cuban President Miguel Díaz-Canel announced on March 13th that he and the US President were in formal talks about solutions to the Cuban energy crisis. This was before the collapse of the grid on the 16th. The negotiations only seem to hasten from here, in my opinion, as the local population begins to show signs of unrest.

The country has effectively been without oil imports for its grid and other energy needs since mid-January, when the Venezuelan lifeline was cut off, as was Mexico's.

The grid may need rebuilding

Here's the thing: while the grid works if the oil inputs are there, it's not logical or clean [from an environmental perspective] to keep the grid the way it is. A transition to natural gas and possibly other energy sources in the future to have a mix would seem probable.

China could see competition

Like Venezuela and other South American countries that may have been dealing with China and its 'belt and road' initiative, this could create a shift and different mix of companies involved on the ground.

Western-aligned electric infrastructure

In my opinion, the easiest route to take is to buy the electric utility and power plant infrastructure names. Narrowing down my list, I identified that there are 4 possible large-cap picks that could be involved in a grid rebuild of Cuba and possibly Venezuela in the future as well.

The screen

This screen was pretty specific. I wanted to nail down high-quality companies, large-cap [my preference for my buy and hold portfolio], and in the grid infrastructure sub-sector. I use EBITDA Yield + Return on Total Capital [ROTC] as my scoring system.

These 4 companies were scored using a version of Joel Greenblatt's Magic Formula to rank them. The higher the score, the better when adding ROTC+EBITDA yield= score.

Company ROTC EBITDA Yield Total Score
Eaton (ETN) 11.30% 4.96% 16.26
Schneider Electric (SBGSY) 9.61% 6.04% 15.65
Siemens Energy (SMEGF) 9.05% 5.36% 14.41
GE Vernova (GEV) 10.65% 2.65% 13.30

While GE Vernova, being an American company, would seem to be the primary beneficiary, it is also the most expensive of the group when using this evaluation system. The truth is, a US company will probably lead a coalition of several subcontractors from around the world if this happens.

Whether the new business demand comes from Cuba, Venezuela, or other nations, this could be a solid group to own not just for conflict rebuilding but also for AI and data center demand.

Market cap sizes

As mentioned, these are all large-cap names, $100 billion+ in size. A sector fund would typically market-cap-weight a fund. Here's what that would look like if market-cap weighted:

  • Total market cap of group = $679.47 Billion

Representation of total market caps:

  • GE Vernova (GEV): 33.5%

  • Schneider Electric (SBGSY): 24.0%

  • Siemens Energy (SMEGF): 21.8%

  • Eaton (ETN): 20.8%

The market cap-weighted portfolio has had a great stretch, but in these scenarios, the backtest length only reaches to Jan 2025, when GE Vernova was spun off. The market-cap-weighted portfolio has had a 96.88% CAGR over the last year. This was mainly due to the strength in the AI data center and utility story overall.

Factor weight

The factor weighting system simply takes each score and then divides it by the total score of all the components. This would make a portfolio geared toward value vs momentum.

Total score = 59.62

Score representation:

  • Eaton (ETN): 27.27%

  • Schneider Electric (SBGSY): 26.24%

  • Siemens Energy (SMEGF): 24.16%

  • GE Vernova (GEV): 22.33%

Over the last year, this portfolio had an 8 percentage point worse performance. However, I would argue a market cap portfolio is backward-looking, as those with the largest market caps probably have the most momentum as well. For an initial theme entry, a value orientation might be a better idea.

Risks and summary

This theme ran up last year, well ahead of any infrastructure rebuild narratives:

Siemens Energy and GE Vernova are the primary return drivers but also pose the most risk here due to pent-up capital gains shareholders have from a short period. This again reinforces a factor weight value tilt versus a market cap weighting entry in my opinion, even though it's tempting to keep running heavy with the winners.

data.title: Charles Schwab Series J Preferreds: Discount Looks Better Than Value
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I rate Charles Schwab's (SCHW) Series J Preferred Shares (SCHW.PR.J) a "Hold." Although SCHW.PR.J (Series J Preferreds thereafter) trades at a steep discount to the redemption price; this discount is not thesis-changing. When factoring in Series J's modest $1.11 annual coupon (distributed quarterly), it seems overvalued compared to other fixed-income alternatives. This "Hold" rating balances SCHW's strong profitability and coverage ratios against Series J overvaluation.

Series D Versus Series J

Both Series D and Series are perpetual, non-cumulative preferreds with solid net income coverage. Both are pari passu with each other, senior to common shares, and subordinated to SCHW's debt. We'll talk about these features in a minute. For now. Let's talk about the share price. Series D trades at $24.8/share, close to the redemption price of $25/share. On the other hand, Series J trades at $18.3/share, a steep discount to the redemption price. All things being constant, investors are better off with a fixed-income security trading at a discount to redemption than one that is not. Series J will become callable at the redemption price next June.

The only reason Series D and Series J trade at different prices is because the coupon rate of each security is different. Since they're in pari passu, their prices adjust to equalize the dividend yield. For example, Series D pays $1.49/unit annually (distributed quarterly). On the other hand, Series J pays $1.11/unit annually (distributed quarterly). The prices of each preferred series adjust so that the yield is almost identical despite paying different coupon rates. Series D yields 5.99% versus 6.06% for Series J.

The Likelihood of Redemption

The probability of SCHW redeeming Series J preferreds is low, in my view. Typically, corporations redeem the preferreds when they need to deleverage or when refinancing becomes cheaper. Financing is now more expensive than when SHCW first issued its Series J preferreds. The preferreds carry a coupon rate of only 4.45%. That's below some US Treasury bonds with longer durations at today's prices. Secondly, if SCHW were to deleverage, it would likely redeem Series D or Series I preferreds. Series D is more expensive for SCHW than Series J, since the coupon rate is about 5.96% ($1.49/share annual coupon payments on $25/share liquidation preference). On the other hand, Series I now carries a fixed-rate coupon, but in June 2026, the rate will reset based on the following formula: New Coupon = 3.168% + 5-year Treasury Rate. This rate resets every 5 years starting June 2026. Based on today's prices, the US 5-Year Treasury rate stands at 3.883%. This means that, starting June 2026, Series I's coupon will be 7.05%, much higher than the coupon rate of either Series D or Series J.

Balance Sheet Refinancing

Series J represents a small portion of SHCW's balance sheet. Total liabilities stood at $442 billion, versus $491 billion in assets as of FY'25 year-end. Series J contributes roughly $600 million to the equity balance (measured at liquidation preference). In total, all preferreds contribute roughly $6.871 billion based on the liquidation preference.

About a third of SCHW's long-term debt is due in the coming twelve months. That's $7.3 billion out of the 22.2 billion of long-term debt. That's not a thesis-changing issue. The company is profitable, and the coverage ratio is extraordinary. We'll discuss this below. In the coming few quarters, it might be useful to keep an eye on this refinancing event. What makes this interesting is that many of the 2026 and 2027 notes were issued when interest rates were low. For example, the $1.25 billion 0.9% notes due March 11, 2026, were likely financed at a much higher rate. The 1.15% Notes due May 2026 will also likely be refinanced at a higher rate. Another example is the 2.45%, 5-year notes issued in March 2022, due March 2027. This rate is very low when juxtaposed against current market prices and will likely be priced higher when the company refinances its debt due.

Is Growth Worth It?

Series J offers about 6.06% dividend, based on an annual coupon payment of $1.11 (distributed quarterly) and a unit price of $18.3/unit as of the time of this writing. On the other hand, SCHW's common shares yield a modest 1.4%. At a 15x forward-adjusted P/E ratio, the earnings yield is about 6.7%. The company's growth is a key factor in this modest common dividend. Reinvesting earnings into the business has helped the company achieve a high score of A on Seeking Alpha's Quant grading system. For investors, the question is whether this trade-off between low income now and higher income in the future is worth it.

SCHW's 5-year average ROE stands at roughly 15%. In other words, for each dollar of equity, SCHW generates 15 cents of net income. With each dollar retained, the equity increases. Thus, SCHW's sustained growth rate is equal to Retention Rate X ROE. In FY'25, net income available to common shareholders stood at $8.4 billion versus $2 billion in dividends. This translates to a payout ratio of 24%, or a retention rate of 76%. Using this figure, the company's sustainable net income growth is about 11.4%. That's an impressive figure when compared to the average brokerage institution. However, assuming a constant share count, an investor buying SCHW today would have to wait 13 years to grow the current $1.28 common dividend to reach the 6% yield offered by Series J preferreds today. ($1.28 × 1.114^14 = $5.802)

Risks

The main risk of investing in Series J Preferreds is their price. The yield is about 6%. That's not much different from the 30-year treasury rate, my preferred benchmark for perpetual preferred securities such as SCHW's Series J. The 30-year treasury rate now stands at 4.9%. A 1.1% risk premium is too small, leaving little upside and a much higher downside.

The upside risk to this Hold thesis is a decision to redeem the preferreds. I don't think this is likely, but starting June 2026, Series J will become callable at SCHW's discretion. Such a scenario would result in substantial gains to the investors, contradicting this "Hold" thesis.

Final Thoughts

SCHW's Series J preferreds look expensive despite trading at a steep discount to the redemption price. The current market rates and the available fixed-income alternatives render this ticker more of a "Hold" than a compelling "Buy." The shares are perpetual, with no clean exit. This, along with the fixed-rate feature, increases the sensitivity of Series J preferreds to changes in interest rates. This is manifested in the 27% decline in their price since inception in 2021. The securities are also non-cumulative, which is yet another point against a "Buy" rating, yet it is worth noting that SCHW needs to continue paying preferred dividends to maintain its reputation and relationships with lenders and the broader investor community.

data.title: CACI International Rightly Disputes Its Industry Classification: Oddly, That's Bullish
data.description:
William Shakespeare was wrong — or at least he didn’t always age well.

In Act II, Scene II of his classic Romeo & Juliet, the Bard has Juliet say:

What’s in a name? That which we call a rose by any other name would smell as sweet.

Try telling that to CACI International (CACI) CEO John Mengucci. He’s peeved about CACI’s industry classification.

I can’t be sure, but I suspect he may be the first CEO ever to have made an issue of this on an earnings call, the 1/22/26 call to be exact:

We have a NAICS, GICS, whatever code you want to call it, that makes us government services company.

(He also discussed his concern, about GICS specifically, at a 3/17/26 conference.)

GICS stands for Global Industry Classification Standard. NAICS stands for North American Industry ClassificationSystem.

GICS, NAICS, and others offer competing protocols for assigning companies to business classifications.

I’m most familiar with GICS. I’ve lived with it for many years at companies for which I worked in the past.

You live with it too since it’s what Seeking Alpha licenses. (To be precise, Seeking Alpha says it uses GICS for “some sector and industry” labels. No matter —Seeking Alpha lives in the world of GICS.)

Based on GICS, Seeking Alpha assigns CACI to the Industrials sector. In a GICS variation, Seeking Alpha puts CACI in an Industry called “Research and Consulting Services.” Strictly speaking this is a GICS sub-industry that’s part of the “Professional Services” industry. For what it’s worth, I think Seeking Alpha made a good choice here in how it applied GICS.

The bigger issue, the one flagged by Mengucci, is whether CACI really belongs in Professional Services or Research and Consulting Services. Based on how CACI’s business has evolved since 2012, and based on the companies cited by Mengucci as peers, I suspect he would rather CACI be categorized under Aerospace & Defense.

I agree with Mengucci.

And contrary to what Shakespeare’s Juliet might say, a Research and Consulting Services stock does not smell as sweet as one classified as Aerospace and Defense.

I say “smell” metaphorically here. But there are investment-oriented numbers that directly play to our financial senses.

Clearly, Aerospace and Defense smells much sweeter to Mr. Market than Research and Consulting Services. (The ratios are higher, but that's because the market expects better things.)

It’s thus intriguing to envision prospects for CACI stock should investors come to see it among the former.

To be fair, GICS isn’t hallucinating. At one time, the company was definitely a Research and Consulting Services company. According to NMS Consulting, this is a business…

in which external advisers help organizations think through difficult questions, design options, and carry out agreed actions. Instead of selling a product or running a standard process, consultants work with management teams on specific topics that need focused attention.

That’s a worthwhile endeavor. CACI calls it “Expertise.”

But CACI has spent more than a decade moving away from it. Today, a business it calls “Technology” contributes about 60% of revenues. And that portion is still rising.

But even this label short changes the sophistication of what CACI is doing and where it’s going.

I have to confess CACI isn’t the easiest company to grasp. So I want to start by…

Banishing Clutter

CACI is involved in a dizzying variety of defense-related programs. And it’s easy for one’s head to spin while trying to keep track of them all. Adding to the burden is the challenge a non-defense expert would face trying to get through an acronym-filled conference- or earning-call transcript without many detours to Google.

I suggest that one refrain from trying to do this. I propose focusing on the forest, not the trees.

Resist, too, the temptation to bog down in Congressional (defense) budget headlines. When high priority projects await, money finds ways to flow through. It can be through regularly budgeted money. It can be through reconciliation funding. (This is a special fast-track approach that is not subject to filibuster.)

And even when overall defense spending is reduced, high priority programs may still get funded in full or nearly in full.

CACI focuses heavily on high priority projects. As a result, it saw very modest by defense-spending standards impacts from DOGE cutbacks and relatively modest (albeit non-zero) impacts from periods of U.S. government shutdowns.

Finally, Mengucci cautioned analysts that CACI is…

not a quarter-to-quarter company. We're a year-to-year and we're going to be a decade-to-decade company.

How CACI Operates

Agile software is a phrase one often encounters in connection with CACI. This refers to a software development process where…

[i]nstead of delivering a finished product all at once (as is conventionally done), Agile breaks work into smaller, manageable cycles called sprints, each producing a functional piece of value that is delivered incrementally. Teams collaborate closely, review progress regularly, and quickly adapt based on feedback, ensuring the final product closely aligns with user expectations.

A typical sprint lasts between 2-4 weeks, allowing for rapid delivery, continuous testing, and frequent course correction. This approach reduces risk, boosts transparency, and encourages stakeholders to guide the product’s direction throughout the project.

At a 3/17/26 Bank of America conference, Mengucci discussed CACI’s version of this:

We have 1,400 people that are co-located with the 5 large combatant commanders all around the globe. That is -- that allows us to have unique access to every minute of every conflict that the U.S. is involved in to understand what technologies are working, which ones aren't, where are the gaps, how are we prosecuting different conflicts, what's the EW (Electronic Warfare) picture look like there?

That's all firsthand knowledge that our 1,400 people…. [W]e get to see that on an hourly basis. That expertise and positions like those folks, whether it's intel analysts, targeting analysts and all provide that constant feedback loop to us. We then invest ahead of customer need using our own balance sheet, not reimbursable through the U.S. government, which then allows us to sell the technology that we create in a commercial-like manner, which is exactly what this administration wants, invest ahead of customer need. We don't place very risky bets because every bet we place on building the tech is well informed by the experts that we have out in the field.

So, unlike conventional consultants that solve problems and then move on to other things, CACI’s tech specialists are embedded full-time with combat commands. And unlike conventional software companies, CACI doesn’t build something to licensee to many customers. It builds something in close collaboration with one user for that user.

No wonder classifying CACI under GICS seems like trying to shove a round peg into a square hole!

What CACI Does

The company organizes its business around seven areas. (These aren’t segments for financial reporting purposes. These are task-oriented groupings.)

  1. C3I — Command, Control, Communications, and Intelligence technology and networks.
  2. Cyber — Addressing cyber threats.
  3. Digital Solutions — Agile-at-scale process automation.
  4. Enterprise IT — Modernizing and sustaining IT ecosystems.
  5. Mission and Engineering Support — Services that help customers achieve a decisive tactical edge.
  6. Space – Helping to “deliver information to and from space along with the protection of these vital space assets using both offensive and defensive capabilities.”
  7. Spectrum Superiority – “[D]ifferentiated technology across the radio-frequency (RF) spectrum for intelligence, surveillance, reconnaissance (ISR), and electronic warfare (EW).”

(Source of information and quoted text: Pages 4-5 of latest 10K)

That’s a nice list.

I particularly want right now to call out the sixth item, “Space.”

CACI has long been a very acquisitive company. But it just big-time upped the ante. On 3/9/25, CACI completed its all-cash $2.6 billion purchase of ARKA Group. The latter…

provides industry-leading electro-optical/infrared (EO/IR) and hyperspectral imaging capabilities, and Agentic AI-based software, that deliver robust geospatial intelligence for critical national security missions.

Given what we’re already seeing with the evolution of drones, missiles, tracking and targeting, it doesn’t take much imagination to see how important ARKA and space are likely to become to CACI.

No doubt ARKA is expensive. It’ll change the company’s balance sheet. (I’ll address that further below.)

As to the income statement, here’s what CFO Jeffrey MacLauchlan told attendees at the 2/18/26 Citi Global Industrial Tech and Mobility Conference:

ARKA will be accretive to our growth rate and our EBITDA margins immediately. We'll be earnings per share neutral in the first full year and accretive in the second. Those statistics that I just gave you don't presume any synergies, either revenue or cost, and we expect to realize some of both.

A Closer Look at Drones

Consider today’s hottest headlines — wars in Ukraine and now Iran (and the Middle East).

The latter isn’t about armies fighting on the ground, or even air forces dropping bombs. Missiles and drones are looming large.

Ukraine experienced a lot of conventional warfare. But lately, drones have been gaining prominence.

Consider too potential future trends in warfare. Ukraine and Iran/the Middle East may be templates for the further evolution of warfare.

Against this backdrop, consider Mengucci’s 3/17/26 remarks regarding CACI and anti-drone activity.

So in the area of drones and counter drones, a lot of field-based experience as to how our drones being flown, whether it's large near peer countries or nation states. And the lesson that those enemies change their tactics every 4 to 8 hours continuously over a 3- or 4-year period. Every 4 or 8 hours, the signature that those threats fly in is completely different than it was 4 hours earlier. So for us to understand the nature, when we say we build counter drone solutions, hundreds of systems, thousands of sensors that are out there with hundreds of realtime confirmed kills in a non-kinetic, low, no collateral manner, that's a calling card that just about every command or everybody in the United States military knows.

And I know that we're in a very busy time where a lot of companies are claiming they have counter UAS solutions. I'm willing to say we probably have some of the best because we have those embedded folks there.

(Emphasis supplied. UAS means Unmanned Aircraft Systems.)

Mengucci later said:

So how far out can you detect that threat that give you time to take the right course of action. We all know the economic side of counter-counter- drone work is we, as a nation, other partner nations cannot spend -- cannot afford to spend $2 million on a missile, take a $30,000 drone down. We've already heard that. If you let that continue, you lose the economic war. Eventually, you lose the overall war.

I have to interrupt Mengucci’s verbal flow here.

Spending $2 million to counter a $300,000 drone! That should give you a sense of how incredibly hard this kind of warfare is.

CACI, addressing, as it does problems like this, isn’t about military brawn (tanks, bombers, fighter jets, bombs, submarines, battle ships, etc.). It’s about military brains.

Remember this later as we contemplate CACI’s stock valuation!

Let’s continue as Mengucci moves into, not just brains, but the need for agility.

[O]ur solutions are at a distance that give commanders 18 minutes of decision time, not 6 seconds. Majority of what you all hear about today is 1 to 3 kilometers on a small Class 1 drone that gives a commander about 6 seconds of decision time, and you're only seeing 1 to 3 kilometers. So it takes you 2 seconds for your mind to react. You got 4 seconds to pick the right button to hit. If you miss it, you're going to going to get hit. So it's great to talk about low-cost drone solutions. There are low-cost drones, but the way to find them in either cellular or satellite-based or using radar, using passive radar, active radar, using RF, there's a multiple number of sensors you need to be able to track these things. Not only track them, but find them and then come up with targeting, you can either pass to a kinetic kill or you can non kinetically take those down.

And let’s not forget today’s favorite topic — AI. Mengucci touched on that too.

So in a software baseline that learns continuously, it's all AI based. So as we find other threats and other cyber bullets that we can send to those drones, we can update everybody's systems concurrently all over the world. So it's a very different scale, a very different level of discrimination and something that is selling very well today and will continue to sell very well.

CACI is about much more than countering drones. Remember the seven-part list presented above.

I featured this today, first because its a timely topic. Also, it vividly illustrates CACI’s special calling card — the kinds of sophisticated things it can do because of the way it embeds its people within military command operations.

The Numbers

When looking at CACI’s numbers, bear in mind that the company’s latest reporting period, ended 12/31/25, predates the very large ARKA acquisition. So don’t try to extrapolate.

But what we see will give us a sense of how CAACI generally performs, bearing in mind that acquisitions are business as usual here.

As to the relative comparisons I usually show, you got a preview above when I showed valuation comparisons.

I’m comparing CACI to medians among companies in two industries — the Research and Consulting Services industry to which GICS assigns it, and the Aerospace and Defense industry, which arguably seems more suitable. I also compare CACI to the portfolio of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). I use medians since these aren’t impacted by wild distortions often caused by unusual data items.

Here are the historic and forward growth rates.

Clearly, CACI fares better than the SPY Group. It’s also better than Research and Consulting Services.

It’s not fully up to speed versus Aerospace and Defense. But as operations labeled Technology by CACI comprise an escalating portion of the business mix, it could gain ground.

Here are the core fundamentals.

This table is likely to change dramatically due to the ARKA acquisition.

That’s especially so in the top four rows which relate to debt.

Returns on Equity and Assets might change a lot in the short term as the capital base quickly grows. But the profit impact should eventually restore the comparisons.

Risk

Operationally, CACI is a veteran. So this kind of risk is no more than average for CACI.

That said, there is likely to be some sentiment risk as headlines relating to defense spending and budgetary battles ebb and flow. Investors may periodically lose track of this company’s long-term story as discussed above.

That shouldn’t happen. But as experienced investors know, there can be short-term differences between how the market should be and how it actually is.

Now, considering the size of the ARKA acquisition, the Financial Statements Highlights table becomes especially interesting.

The Acquisitions line confirms that such activity has long been an important aspect of CACI.

Note, though, that the debt ratio never got out of control, at least as of the time of period-ending reports.

With respect to questions about the sharply increased leverage CACI will take on through the CACI purchase, management responded by pointing out that the company has a track record of bringing such leverage spikes back down to reasonable levels.

This suggests management will do it again following the CACI purchase.

Notice, too, that as acquisitive as CACI has been, it has been a consistent buyer of its own stock. It expects to continue doing so in the future.

Based on CACI’s track record, and my belief that ARKA will turn out to be an excellent addition to CACI, I believe financial risk here to be manageable.

What to do About CACI Stock

I showed you the valuation ratios above. But for your convenience, I’ll show them again here.

Firstly, I think CACI’s Proj. 3-5Y EPS Gr is too low. As the Street becomes more attuned to CACI’s evolution beyond consulting, I expect the projection will move up and approach that which we see for Aerospace and Defense.

Management is working very hard to make this happen by getting its story out into the investment world.

Many companies attend firm-specific conferences. But CACI has hit the road big time. It attended five since it reported December quarter results on 1/22/26. It also attended a conference shortly before the end of that quarter.

So far, the stock hasn’t been reacting.

But that could well change as further results come in after ARKA becomes integrated, and as the Street comes to further appreciate the actual relationship between GICS (consulting — yawn) and reality (the brains behind military power), not to mention global tensions and increasing need for what CACI brings to the table.

Here’s the price chart.

It’s looked good for much of the last 12 months. But the stock seems to be taking a momentum breather right now.

Notice that the 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) and the 50-day EMA both look lackluster.

Consider, too, Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO). Both measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

CO seems to have cooled a bit, as have the moving averages.

The cooling off may reflect a wait-and-see stance regarding the just-completed ARKA purchase. That’s OK. I expect the passage of time will fix that.

But notice the CMF. It’s been noticeably more bullish. Remember, this is an institutions-oriented indicator. Perhaps all the people management has been speaking to lately are listening to, and starting to embrace CACI’s message.

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew “Strong …” extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think “Buy,” Hold,” or “Sell” are enough.

I see CACI as likely outperforming the broader 3- to 5-year market as ARKA contributes and as Mr. Market comes to increasingly understand the kind of company CACI truly is.

I translate that to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by rating CACI as a “Buy.”
